dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - Remote Desktop Services (Terminal Services)

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: Session logon succeeded
entries.summary: RDP session logon succeeded on the target host.
entries.details: Generated on the target host when a user successfully logs on via RDP. This is one of the most reliable indicators of a completed RDP interactive logon. The Address field is present even when NLA is used.

investigationPivots: Pivot on User and Address to trace who connected from where
Correlate SessionID with EID 23/24/25 to track full session lifecycle
Correlate with Security EID 4624 LogonType 10 using username and timestamp
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate admin activity via RDP
Jump server or bastion host connections

keyFields.name: User
keyFields.description: The DOMAIN\username of the user who logged on.

keyFields.name: SessionID
keyFields.description: The session identifier for the RDP session.

keyFields.name: Address
keyFields.description: Source IP address of the connecting client.

detectionRules.title: RDP Session Logon from External or Unexpected Source
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager"
| where EventID == 21
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="User">' User '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Address">' Address '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="SessionID">' SessionID '</Data>' *
| where Address !startswith "10." and Address !startswith "192.168." and Address !startswith "172." and Address != "LOCAL"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, Address, SessionID
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Correlate with Security EID 4624 LogonType 10 for the same User and timestamp. Adjust the IP filter to match your internal network ranges.

detectionRules.title: RDP Session Logon
detectionRules.rule:
title: RDP Session Logon
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: terminalservices-localsessionmanager
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 21
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrator RDP access
  - Bastion host connections
level: low

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: Shell start notification received
entries.details: Generated immediately after EID 21 when the user's shell (explorer.exe or equivalent) starts within the RDP session. Confirms that the logon progressed to a full interactive desktop, not just an authentication handshake. Absence of this event after EID 21 may indicate the session was terminated before the desktop initialized.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 21 using SessionID to confirm full desktop initialization
Missing EID 22 after EID 21 may indicate a failed or aborted session
commonFalsePositives: Normal RDP logon activity

keyFields.name: User
keyFields.description: The user whose shell started.

keyFields.name: SessionID
keyFields.description: The session identifier.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: Session logoff succeeded
entries.details: Generated when a user fully logs off from an RDP session (session is destroyed). Distinct from EID 24 (disconnect): a logoff terminates the session and releases all resources, whereas a disconnect leaves the session running on the server.

investigationPivots: Correlate SessionID with EID 21 to calculate total session duration
Absence of EID 23 with EID 24 present means the session is still running disconnected
commonFalsePositives: Normal end-of-session activity

keyFields.name: User
keyFields.description: The user who logged off.

keyFields.name: SessionID
keyFields.description: The session identifier.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: Session has been disconnected
entries.summary: RDP session disconnected but not logged off.
entries.details: Generated when the RDP client disconnects without logging off -- the session remains running on the server and can be reconnected. A session in the disconnected state is still accessible via the console or a reconnecting RDP client.

investigationPivots: Correlate SessionID with EID 25 to detect reconnection to this disconnected session
Long-lived disconnected sessions with no subsequent EID 23 may indicate persistent attacker access
Address field captures the client IP at time of disconnect
commonFalsePositives: Network interruption causing unintended RDP disconnect
Users who close the RDP window without formally logging off

keyFields.name: User
keyFields.description: The user whose session was disconnected.

keyFields.name: SessionID
keyFields.description: The session identifier.

keyFields.name: Address
keyFields.description: Source IP of the disconnecting client.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: Session reconnection succeeded
entries.summary: RDP client reconnected to an existing disconnected session.
entries.details: Generated when an RDP client successfully reconnects to a previously disconnected session (a session that was preserved after EID 24). Unlike EID 21 (new logon), EID 25 indicates resumption of an existing session without a fresh authentication.

investigationPivots: Reconnections from a different IP than the original session (EID 24 Address) may indicate session hijacking
Correlate SessionID with EID 24 to determine how long the session was disconnected before reconnection
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate user reconnecting after a network drop
Admin resuming an existing session from a different workstation

keyFields.name: User
keyFields.description: The user who reconnected.

keyFields.name: SessionID
keyFields.description: The session identifier being reconnected.

keyFields.name: Address
keyFields.description: Source IP of the reconnecting client.

entries.title: RD Gateway service started
entries.summary: The Remote Desktop Gateway service started successfully.
entries.details: Generated when the Remote Desktop Gateway service starts. This event confirms the RD Gateway is operational. Unexpected restarts of the RD Gateway service -- particularly outside maintenance windows or following configuration changes -- may indicate tampering with the gateway infrastructure.

investigationPivots: Correlate with System log EID 7036 (service state change) for the same service and with any preceding errors or configuration change events

entries.title: RD Gateway connection authorized (CAP passed)
entries.summary: A user met the connection authorization policy and was permitted to connect through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user successfully passes the Connection Authorization Policy (CAP) evaluation at the RD Gateway. CAP controls who is allowed to connect through the gateway (authentication, group membership, device health). This event represents successful external authentication to the RD Gateway and is the primary event for baselining who is accessing the gateway.

investigationPivots: Compare ClientAddress against known VPN egress IPs, corporate office IPs, and user's historical connection locations
Off-hours connections (outside business hours) from unfamiliar IPs for privileged accounts warrant immediate review
High volume of EID 200 events from a single ClientAddress across multiple usernames may indicate credential stuffing success
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate remote workers connecting from home or travel — ClientAddress will vary

keyFields.name: Username
keyFields.description: The authenticated username.

keyFields.name: ClientAddress
keyFields.description: External source IP of the connecting client.

keyFields.name: CAPName
keyFields.description: The Connection Authorization Policy that was satisfied.

entries.title: RD Gateway connection denied (CAP failed)
entries.summary: A user failed the connection authorization policy and was denied access through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user fails the Connection Authorization Policy evaluation at the RD Gateway. CAP failure can occur due to authentication failure (wrong credentials), lack of group membership in the allowed user group, or device health policy failure. This is the primary event for detecting brute-force and credential stuffing attacks against the RD Gateway from the internet. Unlike Security log EID 4625 (which logs failed logons on the internal host), EID 201 is logged on the gateway itself -- visible before authentication reaches any internal system.

investigationPivots: Count EID 201 events per ClientAddress per hour — thresholds above 10-20 per hour from one IP indicate automated spraying
Multiple usernames targeted from a single ClientAddress = credential spray; single username from multiple IPs = distributed brute-force
A sequence of EID 201 events followed by a single EID 200 from the same ClientAddress confirms a successful attack after failures
commonFalsePositives: Users with expired passwords or recently changed passwords attempting to connect before updating credentials
Misconfigured RD Gateway client software sending incorrect authentication material

keyFields.name: Username
keyFields.description: The username that failed authentication.

keyFields.name: ClientAddress
keyFields.description: External source IP of the connection attempt.

keyFields.name: DenialReason
keyFields.description: The reason the connection was denied.

entries.title: RD Gateway connection denied (NAP health policy failed)
entries.summary: A user was denied access through the RD Gateway because their device failed the Network Access Protection health policy.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes authentication but is denied access because their device fails the Network Access Protection (NAP) health policy check configured at the RD Gateway. NAP checks can enforce requirements such as firewall status, AV signature currency, OS patch level, or custom health validators. NAP failures can indicate a user connecting from an unmanaged or out-of-compliance device, or a compromised device that has had security controls (AV, firewall) disabled.

investigationPivots: Identify the health policy that failed — AV disabled or firewall off on a remote device is a significant indicator
Correlate the Username with the device the user normally connects from — a NAP failure from a new device may indicate account compromise
Repeated NAP failures followed by a NAP success from the same ClientAddress may indicate policy bypass attempts
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate users connecting from personal or unmanaged devices not enrolled in MDM
Devices with temporarily expired AV signatures or pending Windows updates

keyFields.name: Username
keyFields.description: The authenticated username.

keyFields.name: ClientAddress
keyFields.description: External source IP of the connection.

keyFields.name: HealthPolicy
keyFields.description: The NAP health policy that the device failed.

entries.title: Listener RDP-Tcp received a connection
entries.summary: RDP listener received an inbound TCP connection.
entries.details: Generated when the RDP listener (RDP-Tcp) accepts an inbound TCP connection on the target host. This is the earliest indicator in the RemoteConnectionManager log that a connection attempt is in progress -- it fires before authentication. The event records the source IP of the connecting client. Because it precedes authentication, it will be generated for both successful and failed connection attempts.

investigationPivots: Correlate source IP with EID 1149 to confirm the connection resulted in successful authentication
EID 261 without a subsequent EID 1149 from the same IP may indicate a port scan, failed NLA negotiation, or brute-force attempt
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate RDP connections from admin hosts
RDP health checks from monitoring infrastructure

keyFields.name: ClientIP
keyFields.description: Source IP address of the connecting client.

entries.title: RD Gateway resource access denied (RAP failed)
entries.summary: A user passed connection authorization but was denied access to the requested internal resource by the resource authorization policy.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes the CAP (connection authorization) but is denied access to a specific internal resource by the Resource Authorization Policy (RAP). RAP controls which internal hosts a user is permitted to reach through the gateway -- limiting lateral movement potential even for authenticated users. This event logs the specific internal host the user attempted to reach, making it a reconnaissance and lateral movement detection event.

investigationPivots: TargetServer reveals which internal host was probed — attempts to reach domain controllers, file servers, or security tools by non-privileged accounts are critical
A sequence of EID 200 (CAP pass) → multiple EID 301 (RAP denies to different hosts) from the same session indicates internal reconnaissance via the gateway
Compare TargetServer against the user's role — accessing systems outside their normal work scope warrants review
commonFalsePositives: Users who have legitimate access to a system but the RAP was not updated to include their new access requirement
IT administrators testing connectivity to new systems before RAP policies are updated

keyFields.name: Username
keyFields.description: The authenticated username.

keyFields.name: ClientAddress
keyFields.description: External source IP.

keyFields.name: TargetServer
keyFields.description: The internal host the user attempted to reach.

entries.title: RD Gateway resource connection succeeded (RAP passed)
entries.summary: A user passed the resource authorization policy and successfully connected to an internal resource through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes both the CAP (EID 200) and the RAP, and successfully establishes a connection to an internal resource through the gateway. This is the end-to-end success event: the user authenticated, was authorized by connection policy, authorized by resource policy, and reached their destination host. This event pair -- EID 200 + EID 302 -- is the primary baseline for RD Gateway activity.

investigationPivots: TargetServer identifies which internal host was accessed — combine with TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager EID 21 on the target host to confirm session establishment
Build a baseline of {Username → TargetServer} pairs; connections to new TargetServers for an account indicate scope expansion
ClientAddress pivot: the same external IP connecting as multiple different users is a strong credential sharing or compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate remote work generating connections outside normal business hours
IT staff accessing multiple servers through the gateway during incident response

keyFields.name: Username
keyFields.description: The authenticated username.

keyFields.name: ClientAddress
keyFields.description: External source IP of the connecting client.

keyFields.name: TargetServer
keyFields.description: The internal host successfully reached through the gateway.

entries.title: RDP ClientActiveX is trying to connect to the server
entries.summary: Outbound RDP connection attempt initiated from this host.
entries.details: Generated on the source (client) machine when an outbound RDP connection attempt is initiated. This is a source-side artifact -- it records where this machine connected to, making it critical for tracing lateral movement chains. During an investigation of a compromised host, EID 1024 entries reveal which other systems were targeted for RDP from this machine.

investigationPivots: The server field identifies the RDP target — pivot to that host's LocalSessionManager and RemoteConnectionManager logs
Multiple EID 1024 entries to different targets from the same host indicate lateral movement
Correlate timestamp with LocalSessionManager EID 21 on the target host to confirm a successful session was established
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate admin activity from IT or helpdesk machines
Jump server connections to managed endpoints

keyFields.name: Server
keyFields.description: The target hostname or IP address of the RDP connection.

entries.title: RDP ClientActiveX has been disconnected
entries.summary: Outbound RDP session ended or was disconnected.
entries.details: Generated on the source (client) machine when an RDP session is disconnected. Common reason codes: 0 (local disconnect/user closed), 1 (remote disconnection by server), 2 (transport-level error), 3 (connection lost/network issue), 260 (DNS name lookup failed).

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 1024 using the server field to calculate session duration
Reason code 2 or 3 (transport error / network loss) may indicate the connection was forcibly terminated
Reason code 260 (DNS failure) following EID 1024 indicates the target hostname did not resolve
commonFalsePositives: Normal end-of-session disconnects by legitimate users

keyFields.name: Server
keyFields.description: The target host of the disconnected RDP session.

keyFields.name: ReasonCode
keyFields.description: Disconnect reason code indicating how the session ended.

entries.title: The client has initiated a multi-transport connection to the server
entries.summary: RDP multi-transport connection established; target IP recorded.
entries.details: Generated on the source (client) machine when an RDP multi-transport connection is initiated. Unlike EID 1024 which may contain a hostname, EID 1102 typically records the resolved IP address, making it useful for cases where EID 1024 only logged a hostname. Not generated for all RDP connections -- depends on whether multi-transport (UDP or additional TCP channels) is negotiated.

investigationPivots: Use the IP address in the server field when EID 1024 only recorded a hostname — helps correlate with network logs
Not all RDP sessions generate this event — absence does not rule out an RDP connection
Correlate IP with network flow data or firewall logs to confirm the connection
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate RDP session that uses multi-transport

keyFields.name: Server
keyFields.description: IP address of the RDP target server.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services: User authentication succeeded
entries.summary: RDP user authentication succeeded; source IP recorded.
entries.details: Generated on the target host when a user successfully authenticates to RDP, including via Network Level Authentication (NLA). This event is one of the most valuable for RDP forensics because it captures the source IP even when NLA is used and the connection precedes an interactive logon. The absence of a domain (Param2 is blank) may indicate a local account logon.

investigationPivots: Pivot on Param3 (source IP) to identify the origin of the RDP connection
Blank Param2 (domain) indicates a local account; non-blank indicates a domain account
Correlate Param1 (username) and timestamp with LocalSessionManager EID 21 to confirm session establishment
EID 1149 without a subsequent LocalSessionManager EID 21 may indicate authentication succeeded but the session was not fully initialized
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate admin or user RDP sessions
Jump server or bastion host activity

keyFields.name: Param1
keyFields.description: The username that authenticated.

keyFields.name: Param2
keyFields.description: The domain of the authenticating user (blank for local accounts).

keyFields.name: Param3
keyFields.description: Source IP address of the connecting client.

entries.title: Remote Desktop Services accepted a new TCP connection from client
entries.summary: RDP service accepted and assigned an incoming TCP connection.
entries.details: Generated when the Remote Desktop Services stack accepts a new inbound TCP connection and assigns it to a connection handler. This event fires in the same flow as EID 261 but is generated by a different component and provides the full IP:port tuple. Not all connection attempts produce this event.

investigationPivots: ClientIP field includes the ephemeral port — strip the port to get the source IP for correlation with EID 1149
Cross-reference source IP with network flow data (e.g., firewall or NetFlow logs) to build a full connection picture
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate RDP connections
Network scanner probing TCP 3389

keyFields.name: ClientIP
keyFields.description: Source IP and ephemeral port of the connecting client (e.g., 192.168.1.10:54321).
